title: Competitive Analysis
source: Starter KB compiled from trusted strategy + UX research guidance. Primary references: NN/g competitive usability evaluations and competitive research methods; HBR competitive strategy frameworks (e.g., Porter); Strategyzer value proposition tools; plus web standards for structured comparisons. Example refs: https://www.nngroup.com/articles/competitive-usability-evaluations/ ; https://www.nngroup.com/videos/competitive-reviews-vs-competitive-research/ ; https://hbr.org/2008/01/the-five-competitive-forces-that-shape-strategy ; https://hbr.org/2007/11/mapping-your-competitive-position ; https://www.strategyzer.com/library/the-value-proposition-canvas
purpose: Provide reference knowledge for conducting competitive analysis for product and UX: identifying direct/indirect competitors, benchmarking user experiences, comparing capabilities and positioning, and turning findings into actionable opportunities and design requirements.
scope: Applies to product and UX competitive work: landscape mapping, feature/experience benchmarking, competitive usability evaluations, positioning analysis, and opportunity identification. Excludes copying competitors blindly, IP-sensitive reverse engineering, and any guidance to implement deceptive/dark patterns (use the Dark & Deceptive Practices KB for detection/remediation only).
definitions: A product that targets the same user segments and solves the same core job with a similar solution category.
A different solution category that satisfies the same underlying job (e.g., spreadsheets as a substitute for a workflow tool).
A fast expert review of competitors' UI/flows to learn patterns, strengths/weaknesses, and design conventions; lower rigor than research.
A structured study comparing products using tasks, participants, and measures to assess relative UX strengths/weaknesses. (NN/g competitive usability evaluations)
A reference comparison point for performance, UX quality, features, or outcomes (e.g., onboarding completion time).
A map that compares offerings along meaningful dimensions (e.g., price vs benefits) from the customer's perspective. (HBR positioning map)
A structured table comparing capabilities across competitors, ideally paired with depth/quality ratings and evidence.
A step-by-step analysis of a product flow (e.g., sign-up, search, checkout) capturing patterns, copy, IA, friction, and differentiators.
rulesConstraints: Start with the user job: define the primary job-to-be-done, key tasks, and success outcomes before comparing products.
Separate observation from interpretation: record what you see (evidence) distinctly from your conclusions.
Use comparable tasks and conditions across products (same user intent, same device context) to avoid biased comparisons.
Do not optimize for feature count alone. Evaluate quality, usability, discoverability, and effectiveness.
When doing a competitive usability evaluation, use consistent tasks and scoring criteria to compare relative strengths/weaknesses (NN/g competitive usability).
Triangulate sources: combine UI review with user feedback, public documentation, pricing, and measurable behaviors when available.
Frame findings as opportunities: identify where users struggle, what competitors do better/worse, and what differentiates meaningfully.
Avoid “copying”: replicate the underlying principle, not the surface UI. Consider brand, platform guidelines, and your constraints.
Document assumptions and uncertainty. If you cannot verify a claim (e.g., behind a paywall feature), label it as unknown.
Update cadence: treat competitive findings as time-bound; include an updatedAt date and consider periodic refresh for key markets.

do: Define scope upfront: products, segments, platforms (iOS/Android/web), and top 3-7 tasks to compare.
Create a consistent template for capture: screenshots, flow steps, notes, plus a rubric (e.g., clarity, friction, time, errors).
Classify competitors: direct, adjacent, substitutes, and “best-in-class” inspirations for specific tasks.
Compare at the right level: user outcomes and task success first, then patterns and UI components.
Use a rubric to score experiences (e.g., onboarding clarity, navigation, forms, accessibility, states).
Assess positioning: who it's for, key promises, pricing model, and what the product emphasizes on landing pages and in-app.
Identify differentiators and “table stakes” separately to avoid chasing parity features unnecessarily.
Capture both strengths and weaknesses so design decisions are balanced and grounded.
dont: Don't treat competitive analysis as a list of features; assess the user experience quality and business model constraints.
Don't assume a competitor's implementation is optimal just because it's common; validate with user needs and context.
Don't include or recommend deceptive/dark patterns; if found, document harm and remediation only.
Don't ignore accessibility and states (loading, empty, error, permissions) in comparisons; these often differentiate real quality.
Don't base conclusions on marketing claims alone; verify with product use and evidence when possible.
Don't overfit to one competitor; use multiple references to avoid biased direction.

examples: Flow: Sign-up → onboarding → first success action. Capture: steps, user questions, friction points, copy patterns, error handling, and time-to-value.
Capability | Competitor A | Competitor B | Our product | Notes/Evidence | Quality (1-5) | User value (H/M/L) | Differentiation (H/M/L).
Target user? Core promise? Proof points? Pricing model? Key objections addressed? What's emphasized in nav and hero content?
Competitors reduce onboarding time-to-value by guiding a single primary setup path; opportunity: simplify our onboarding to one clear path with progressive disclosure.
Task 1: Find X; Task 2: Configure Y; Task 3: Recover from an error; compare success rate, time, confidence, and reported difficulty across products.
edgeCases: Paywalled/enterprise features: document as unknown unless verified; avoid assumptions about capability depth.
Rapidly changing markets (AI features, pricing): separate stable findings (IA, core flow quality) from volatile ones (feature availability) and refresh more often.
Platform differences: compare like with like (iOS vs iOS). If one competitor is web-only, note platform constraints explicitly.
Different target segments: if a competitor targets a different persona, treat it as inspiration rather than direct benchmark; adjust rubrics accordingly.
Legal/compliance constraints: note where your product must differ due to regulation, privacy, or enterprise requirements.
Copying risk. When a pattern is valuable, abstract it into principles (reduce steps, clarify hierarchy, improve recovery) rather than replicating UI exactly.
